content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF

PILLSBURY, MADISON & SUTRO
POST OFFICE BOX 7880

OTHER OFFICES
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA
SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA
SAN JOSE, CALIFORNIA
WALNUT CREEK, CALIFORNIA
WASHINGTON, D.C.

SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94120

TELEPHONE (415) 983-1000

CABLE ADDRESS "EVANS"
TELEX 34743
TELECOPIER (415) 398-2096

WRITER'S OFFICE AND
DIRECT DIAL NUMBER

235 Montgomery Street
(415) 983-7272

March 30, 1990


Kathryn Donovan, Esq.
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Kathy:

     This letter constitutes a request for informal
written advice pursuant to Government Code § 83114(b).

     This firm represents political committees sponsored
by corporations. From time to time, questions arise concern-
ing the administration of such committees under the campaign
disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974, as
amended (the "Act") and regulations promulgated thereunder by
the Commission. Our question is whether a corporate employee
who delivers a campaign contribution to a recipient candidate
or committee on behalf of a political committee sponsored by
his or her corporate employer qualifies as an "intermediary"
under the Act.

     The Act defines an intermediary as a person who
delivers to a candidate or committee a contribution from
another person unless such contribution is from the person's
employer, immediate family or an association to which the
person belongs. Gov't Code § 84302.5; 2 Cal.Code Regs.
§ 18432.5 (Adopted).

     In the past, we have received informal advice that
if an employee of a PAC sponsor delivers a PAC check, he or
she would not qualify as an intermediary. On the other hand,
we recently have been informed that the FPPC's staff may have
changed its position that employees of a PAC sponsor who
deliver PAC contributions should now be treated as
intermediaries.

Kathryn Donovan, Esq.
March 30, 1990
Page 2

We believe that a corporate employee who delivers a
corporate PAC check should not be treated as an intermediary
for the following reasons.

First, the statutory definition of "intermediary"
specifically excludes members of an association from its
coverage. Consistent with this exemption, employees of a
corporation who contribute to the PAC, or qualify as PAC
members under PAC rules should be treated as members of the
corporation's PAC. As "members" of an association, the
employees should not qualify as intermediaries.

Second, one can look for guidance from the framers
of Proposition 68. As we understand it, this definition was
intended to provide for additional disclosure by so-called
"bundlers"--persons who collect contributions from various
sources and deliver them en masse to a favored candidate or
committee. An interpretation of "intermediary" to require
disclosure of corporate employees who deliver their PAC
checks, but not those who deliver contributions from corpo-
rate treasury funds, is both inconsistent with the purpose of
the statute and provides little additional information to the
public.

If you have any questions, please do not hesitate
to contact me.

Very truly yours,

Teresa Craigie Lahaderne

cc: Mr. F. K. Lowell
    Mr. E. W. LeBaron, Jr.
    Mr. A. A. Andersen
    Mr. B. W. Maas


10334134

sections.question: Our question is whether a corporate employee who delivers a campaign contribution to a recipient candidate or committee on behalf of a political committee sponsored by his or her corporate employer qualifies as an "intermediary" under the Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a corporate employee who delivers a campaign contribution on behalf of a corporate-sponsored political committee qualify as an "intermediary" under the Political Reform Act, requiring additional disclosure?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from a law firm to the FPPC, not an FPPC response. No CONCLUSION section present. The document provides the requestor's legal arguments and position but awaits FPPC's advice. Document is dated March 30, 1990.

embedding.summary: Law firm representing corporate political committees requests informal written advice on whether corporate employees delivering PAC contributions should be treated as intermediaries under the Political Reform Act's disclosure requirements.
